burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT

cross_connections.display_label: was his father-in-law; US Rep NY

narrative_blocks.header: US Congressman from Connecticut, 1851-1855
narrative_blocks.body: Colin Macrae Ingersoll served two terms as a United States Congressman from Connecticut's 2nd district from March 4, 1851 through March 3, 1855 — succeeding Walter Booth, succeeded by John Woodruff. He continued the Ingersoll family's third generation in Congress alongside his father Ralph Isaacs Ingersoll (CT Congressman 1825-1833) and his cousin Charles Jared Ingersoll of Philadelphia. Connecticut had three concurrent Ingersolls in mid-nineteenth-century politics including his brother Charles R. Ingersoll as Governor.

narrative_blocks.header: Secretary of legation St Petersburg during father's ministry
narrative_blocks.body: Colin served as Secretary of the United States legation at St. Petersburg Russia 1847-1848 — during his father Ralph Isaacs Ingersoll's term as US Minister to Russia under President Polk. In 1848 he served as acting Chargé d'Affaires at the St Petersburg post. The Ingersoll father-son diplomatic Russia posting was a rare American family pattern, taking both generations into the Tsarist court.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Julia Harriet Pratt, daughter of Zadock Pratt
narrative_blocks.body: Colin married Julia Harriet Pratt in 1853 — daughter of US Representative Zadock Pratt of New York, the tanner-banker who built the world's largest tannery at Prattsville NY in 1824. The marriage united two American congressional families. Their four children included future US Minister to Siam George Pratt Ingersoll (1861-1927).

narrative_blocks.header: Died age eighty-four pneumonia, same year as brother
narrative_blocks.body: Colin Macrae Ingersoll died September 13, 1903 in New Haven Connecticut at age eighty-four — the same year his brother Charles Roberts Ingersoll (Governor of Connecticut 1873-1877) also died in January 1903. The two Ingersoll brothers were both interred at Grove Street Cemetery New Haven. The cause of death was pneumonia.

institutions.notes: Yale College + Yale Law School graduate 1839
Trinity College 1839; founding member Phi Kappa Society

father.n: Baron Jan Cornelis Van den Heuvel

parents.mother_research_notes: 3rd wife of Zadock Pratt; died Feb 5 1834 age 26 from childbirth complications

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT

notable.notable_blurb: US Minister to Siam under Wilson; Trinity College + Yale Law

burial.plot_notes: Fairlawn Cemetery, Ridgefield CT (one of four incorporators of the cemetery 1908)

cross_connections.display_label: New Haven law firm founder, lifelong family friend
cross_connections.notes: Both at New Haven bar 1885-1910. Louis was Charles R. Ingersoll's lifelong friend; George Pratt was Charles R.'s nephew (son of Colin M.). Family-anchored bar peers across two generations.

narrative_blocks.header: US Minister to Siam under Wilson, 1917-1918
narrative_blocks.body: In August 1917 President Woodrow Wilson selected George Pratt Ingersoll as US Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary to Siam (now Thailand) — serving August 8, 1917 to June 23, 1918 during WWI. He had retired from law practice earlier in 1917 to focus on public service. Three generations of Ingersolls held principal US diplomatic posts: grandfather Ralph (Minister to Russia 1846-48), father Colin M. (Secretary of legation St Petersburg 1847-48), George Pratt (Minister to Siam 1917-18).

narrative_blocks.header: Vice Chair National Democratic Committee, 1914
narrative_blocks.body: George Pratt Ingersoll served as Vice Chair of the National Democratic Committee in 1914 — the party leadership role during President Wilson's first term. The same year he was appointed prosecuting attorney for Fairfield County Connecticut. In 1910 he had been an unsuccessful Democratic candidate for the US House of Representatives from Connecticut, and later declined to run for the United States Senate.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Alice Witherspoon, John Witherspoon descendant
narrative_blocks.body: On November 3, 1891 at Trinity Church New Haven, George married Alice Witherspoon — daughter of Rev. Orlando Witherspoon of Buffalo NY and a documented descendant of founding father JOHN WITHERSPOON (signer of the Declaration of Independence). The marriage connected the Ingersoll family directly to one of the principal American founding lineages. The couple had two children: Collin M. and Gertrude M.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale Law 1885, three-state practice, trust estates specialty
narrative_blocks.body: George earned his Trinity College BA in 1883, Yale Law School LLB in 1885, and Trinity MA in 1885 — admitted to the Connecticut and New York bars the same year, with a legal specialty in trust estates. He practiced across three CT/NY firms: Murray Ingersoll Hodge & Humphrey (22 William Street NYC), Tyler Ingersoll & Morgan (New Haven), and Cummings & Lockwood (Stamford from October 1914). He served as US Commissioner for Connecticut from 1889.

narrative_blocks.header: Born 1861 New Haven, buried Fairlawn Ridgefield
narrative_blocks.body: George Pratt Ingersoll was born April 24, 1861 in New Haven Connecticut and died February 24, 1927 at Stamford Hospital after two months of illness at age sixty-five. He is buried at Fairlawn Cemetery Ridgefield Connecticut — one of the four original incorporators of the cemetery on October 30, 1908. His residence was 67 Glenbrook Road Stamford, with a summer home in Ridgefield called Ingleside.

institutions.notes: Trinity College BA 1883 + MA 1885; Delta Psi fraternity

notable.notable_blurb: US Minister to Russia under Polk; Congressman 1825-33

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT, 3 Maple Ave East

narrative_blocks.header: US Minister to Russia under President Polk
narrative_blocks.body: Ralph Isaacs Ingersoll was appointed by Democratic President James K. Polk as the sixteenth United States Minister Plenipotentiary to the Russian Empire on August 8, 1846. He presented his credentials in Russia on May 30, 1847, and served until his resignation on July 1, 1848. The appointment was unsought and unexpected — he served his government 'faithfully and ably' before gladly returning to his New Haven law practice.

narrative_blocks.header: Four consecutive Congressional terms 1825-1833
narrative_blocks.body: Ralph served four consecutive terms in the United States Congress representing Connecticut at-large from March 4, 1825 through March 3, 1833 — first elected as an Adams candidate, then reelected as Anti-Jacksonian. He served on the powerful Ways and Means committee for four years, holding the second-ranking position during his final term. He was not a candidate for renomination in 1832.

narrative_blocks.header: Declined US Senate seat from Governor Edwards, 1835
narrative_blocks.body: In 1835 Governor Henry W. Edwards offered Ralph the United States Senate seat made vacant by the death of Senator Nathan Smith. He peremptorily declined — though the legislature would undoubtedly have elected him to the position. He similarly declined repeated gubernatorial nominations from his party across many years, departing from his settled purpose only once when Polk appointed him to Russia.

narrative_blocks.header: Best public speaker his contemporaries ever knew
narrative_blocks.body: One of Ralph's contemporaries with wide experience said of him 'he was the best public speaker I ever knew.' His voice was pleasant and almost musical, with unusual compass — distinct in its lowest tones. He was eminently persuasive before a jury or popular assembly, with a documented personal magnetism that allowed him to sway crowds, by turns humorous and pathetic, sarcastic when needed.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1808, New Haven law, Grove Street burial
narrative_blocks.body: Ralph graduated from Yale College in 1808 and read law for two years with Seth P. Staples before opening his New Haven practice. He married Margaret Catherine Eleanor Van den Heuvel in 1814 and they had seven children together — including future Governor of Connecticut Charles Roberts Ingersoll and US Congressman Colin Macrae Ingersoll. He died in New Haven August 26, 1872 at age eighty-three, buried at Grove Street Cemetery.

institutions.notes: Studied at Yale Law School after Yale College 1808

parents.mother_research_notes: Daughter of NY landowner Charles Ward Apthorp

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT

Lieutenant Governor, Connecticut
political_offices.notes: 9th Lieutenant Governor; elected annually until death

notable.notable_blurb: Connecticut Lieutenant Governor 1816-1823, Superior Court Judge

cross_connections.display_label: Both buried Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven — Hooker descendants

narrative_blocks.header: Lieutenant Governor of Connecticut, 1816-1823
narrative_blocks.body: Jonathan Ingersoll Jr. served as Lieutenant Governor of Connecticut from 1816 until his death in 1823 — the role making him a principal political figure of the early-Republic era in the state. The Lt Governor's office in Connecticut was a significant constitutional post during the years following the 1818 Constitution. His son Ralph I. Ingersoll later described him as 'a gentleman of great moral worth, and among the foremost in his profession.'

narrative_blocks.header: State Attorney, Superior Court Judge, then Lt Governor
narrative_blocks.body: Jonathan rose through three principal Connecticut public-service offices in succession — first State Attorney, then a Judge of the Superior Court, then Lieutenant Governor. The progression took him from prosecutorial to judicial to political leadership across the late-eighteenth and early-nineteenth centuries. His judicial reputation rested on the same qualities of moral worth and professional standing that distinguished his political tenure.

narrative_blocks.header: Father of US Minister to Russia Ralph Ingersoll
narrative_blocks.body: Jonathan's son Ralph I. Ingersoll (born February 8, 1789) followed him into the law and the public service — eight years in the United States Congress (1825-1833), State Attorney for twelve years, Connecticut Minister Plenipotentiary to the Russian court 1846-1848 under President Polk. The Ingersoll legal dynasty was one of the longest-running Connecticut family practices of the nineteenth century. A grandson Charles Roberts Ingersoll became Governor of Connecticut 1873-1877.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT

notable.notable_blurb: Dutch Governor of Demerara 1765-1770; NYC merchant

narrative_blocks.header: Dutch Governor of Demerara 1765-1770
narrative_blocks.body: Jan Cornelis Van den Heuvel served as Governor of the Dutch province of Demerara (in present-day Guyana) from 1765 to 1770 — succeeding Laurens Lodewijk van Bercheijk and preceded Paulus van Schuylenburgh. Demerara was at the time one of the Dutch West India Company's principal sugar-producing colonial territories in South America. After his five-year governorship he relocated to New York City and continued as a merchant with the Dutch West India Company.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter Margaret married Ralph Isaacs Ingersoll, 1814
narrative_blocks.body: Jan Cornelis's daughter Margaret Catherine Eleanor Van den Heuvel (1788-1878) married Ralph Isaacs Ingersoll in 1814 — bringing the Dutch colonial governor's bloodline into the Connecticut Ingersoll-Hooker tree. Through Margaret he became grandfather of seven, including Yale lawyer John Van den Heuvel Ingersoll (drowned Lake Erie 1846), US Rep Colin Macrae Ingersoll, and Governor of Connecticut Charles Roberts Ingersoll. The Van den Heuvel name persisted in Ingersoll descendants for multiple generations.

narrative_blocks.header: Hamilton, Astor, and Halleck grandchildren elsewhere
narrative_blocks.body: Beyond the Ingersoll-Hooker line, Jan Cornelis was grandfather (through daughter Maria) to fourteen Hamilton grandchildren — children of Maria's husband John Church Hamilton (son of Alexander Hamilton). These included Civil War General Alexander Hamilton (1815-1907), Schuyler Hamilton (1822-1903 of the Mexican War), and Elizabeth Hamilton (1831-1884) who married Henry Wager Halleck. Through daughter Susan he was also grandfather to Charlotte Augusta Gibbes who married John Jacob Astor III.

Allen Butler Talcott (T01459) diploma 1890; Charles Hooker Talcott (T01460) 1891. INST155.name: Yale Law School
INST155.notes: One of the principal American law schools. Affiliated with Yale University, New Haven CT, since 1824. Among Hooker-tree affiliates: Ralph I. Ingersoll (1808), John Hooker, Louis H. Bristol (1859), John Wolcott Bristol (1879), Thomas Hooker Jr. (1906), Colin M. Ingersoll, George Pratt Ingersoll (LLB 1885).
